system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:58 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

### Status Review — 2026-03-06 (Roscoe AI)

**Case is post-discovery and in active negotiation.** Landmarks updated to reflect actual progress.

**Key milestones completed:**
- Plaintiff depositions (Cecelia & Greg Neltner) — 9/17/2024 at Richardson Law Group, Ft. Mitchell
- Settlement demand sent — 11/25/2024 ($5,000 for Cecelia's claim)
- $500 nuisance offer received from Progressive BI — 1/22/2025
- Defendant Trinity Cohara deposed — 3/21/2025 at Richardson Law Group, Ft. Mitchell
- Defense subpoenaed additional medical records (Christ Hospital, TriHealth) — 3/7/2025

**Current posture:**
- $5,000 demand vs. $500 offer — significant gap
- Defense cited liability dispute as basis for low offer; firm refused to negotiate further until defendant was deposed
- Defendant has now been deposed; negotiation should resume
- No mediation has been scheduled (was flagged for review 5/31/2024 but never set)
- Cecelia texted "What time is the meeting?" on 7/8/2025 — unclear if this referenced a mediation or other meeting

**Injuries:** Airbag burns/chemical rash on left forearm (primary), temporary neck/back soreness (resolved). Rash healed with mild residual scarring. Cecelia also reported headaches starting ~June 2024 but has not sought treatment for them. Pre-existing: pituitary adenoma (unrelated).

**Next steps needed:**
1. Follow up on negotiations with defense counsel post-defendant deposition
2. Consider scheduling mediation
3. Evaluate whether headache complaints warrant additional medical evaluation to support higher demand

**Defense counsel:** Katie Nelson Dunn, Richardson Law Group PLLC, Ft. Mitchell, KY

# VA (Veterans Affairs) Lien Management

> When a PI client is a veteran receiving care through the VA healthcare system, the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs asserts a federal lien for the cost of accident-related treatment under the Federal Medical Care Recovery Act (42 U.S.C. § 2651). VA liens are managed by the VA Office of General Counsel (OGC) Revenue Law Group — a specialized federal legal team. This process is fundamentally different from private health insurance liens and requires distinct handling.

## Pattern

The VA lien lifecycle is significantly more complex and slower than private insurer liens:

1. **Initial Lien Request**: Firm mails an initial lien request to VA Healthcare early in file setup. This must be sent to the correct VA facility (e.g., VA Medical Center Lexington).
2. **Case Assignment**: VA OGC Revenue Law Group assigns a Paralegal Specialist who becomes the single point of contact. Cases are tracked with a GCL case number (e.g., "596-W-20231106-115744/GCL #537467").
3. **Claim Assertion**: The VA Revenue Law Group asserts a specific dollar amount for accident-related treatment (e.g., "$4,437.13 is the VA's claim in this case").
4. **Records vs. Billing Separation**: The VA separates records requests from billing requests. Records must come from the VA facility; billing is handled by OGC. Sending a records request to the billing fax line (or vice versa) causes delays.
5. **Case Closure and Reopening**: VA cases can be "closed" (with payment issued to the VA) and then need to be "reopened" if additional treatment dates are discovered. The VA case manager will request a list of treatment dates to evaluate reopening.
6. **PIP Payment of VA Lien**: PIP carriers can be directed to pay the VA lien directly. In one case, the PIP carrier issued payment to the Department of Veterans Affairs for the lien amount upon receiving a direction-to-pay with the VA billing statement.
7. **Settlement Integration**: The VA lien must be satisfied from settlement proceeds. The VA expects "full reimbursement" plus any additional new-money settlement.

### Key Differences from Private Health Insurance Liens

| Aspect | Private Health Insurance | VA Lien |
|--------|------------------------|---------|
| Legal basis | State subrogation law / ERISA | Federal Medical Care Recovery Act (42 U.S.C. § 2651) |
| Contact | Claims/subrogation department | OGC Revenue Law Group (Paralegal Specialist) |
| Tracking | Claim number | GCL case number |
| Response time | Days to weeks | Weeks to months |
| Records access | Via provider records dept | Must go through VA facility; veteran can also use MyHealtheVet portal |
| Negotiation | Percentage reductions common | Federal government — less flexibility |
| Case lifecycle | Stays open until resolved | Can be closed and must be reopened |

## Evidence

- In Case D, the initial lien request was mailed to VA Healthcare on day 1 of file setup (July 2023). It took until July 2024 — a full year — before the VA's Revenue Law Group Paralegal Specialist responded with the lien amount of $4,437.13. During that year, multiple follow-up requests were required.
- The VA case was then closed in August 2024 when PIP paid the VA lien. However, when additional VA treatment was discovered later, the firm had to request the case be reopened. The VA case manager asked for "a list of dates you are including" before reopening.

# Litigation Process Patterns

> When PI cases proceed to litigation, they enter a structured legal process involving complaint filing, discovery (interrogatories, document production), depositions, motions practice, and potential mediation or trial. Litigation dramatically increases case complexity, timeline, and administrative burden on the paralegal team.

## Pattern

The litigation lifecycle follows a predictable sequence:

### 1. Complaint Filing
- Draft complaint with interrogatories (IROG)
- File with the circuit court
- Check service of process on defendant
- Court assigns case number and division

### 2. Discovery Phase
- **Interrogatories**: Written questions exchanged between parties
- **Supplemental responses**: As new information becomes available, supplemental discovery responses must be filed
- **Document production**: Medical records, tax returns, employment records
- **Subpoenas**: Third-party records (e.g., bank records from Hometown Bank) obtained via subpoena
- **Timeline**: Court sets discovery deadlines; joint motions to extend are common

### 3. Motions Practice
- Motions to compel (when opposing party doesn't respond to discovery)
- Motions to withdraw counsel
- Joint motions for deadline extensions
- Motion hearings may be remote or rescheduled

### 4. Mediation
- Typically scheduled after discovery is substantially complete
- Mediator selected through national organizations (e.g., NADN)
- Both parties must agree on mediator and date
- Half-day sessions are common; can be via Zoom

# Negotiation After Demand

> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.

## Pattern

### Post-Demand Sequence
1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation

### Client Rejection Pattern
A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties

### Adjuster Follow-Up
- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery

### Communication Challenges
- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase

## Evidence

# Premises Liability Case Patterns

> Premises liability (PL) cases — typically slip-and-fall incidents at commercial properties — follow a different trajectory than auto accident cases. They are more likely to proceed to litigation, involve property owner defense counsel early, and require more extensive expert work (FCE, IME, vocational/economic experts). The liability analysis focuses on property owner notice and maintenance rather than traffic fault.

## Pattern

PL cases differ from auto accident cases in several key ways:

### Liability Analysis
- **Property owner notice**: Was the hazardous condition known to the owner? How long did it exist?
- **Maintenance records**: Were inspection and maintenance protocols followed?
- **Incident reports**: Was an incident report filed at the time of the fall?
- **Surveillance footage**: Commercial properties may have video of the incident; preservation requests should be sent immediately
- **Third-party defendants**: Banks, stores, and other commercial entities typically have dedicated defense counsel (not insurance adjusters)

### Litigation Tendency
- PL cases are more likely to proceed to litigation than auto accident cases
- Defense counsel for commercial property owners may aggressively litigate through discovery, depositions, and motions practice
- Mediation is commonly attempted before trial

### Expert Work
- **Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE)**: Used to document physical limitations from the injury
- **Independent Medical Examinations (IME)**: May be requested by either side; amendments may be needed as treatment progresses
- **Economist/Vocational Reports**: When the injury affects earning capacity, vocational economics experts are retained

### Timeline
- PL cases tend to run significantly longer than auto accident cases (3-6+ years observed vs. 1-2 years for typical MVA)
- Discovery deadlines, motions practice, and mediation scheduling extend the timeline

## Evidence

- In a premises liability case (slip-and-fall at a commercial bank, 2019 incident), the case progressed through litigation with complaint filing, interrogatories (IROG), discovery, depositions, mediation scheduling, IME, FCE, and economist report requests — spanning 2019-2025+. Defense counsel for the property owner actively participated in discovery and motions practice. Mediation was scheduled then cancelled. The case required certified medical records for litigation (not just demand copies).
- The same case involved multiple medical providers across orthopedics, neurosurgery, PCP, and imaging facilities, with the attorney directing additional imaging (MRI) based on IME findings.

# Companion Case Management

> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.

## Pattern

1. **Intake**: One family member signs up first; firm discovers additional family members during the welcome call or initial contact
2. **File Creation**: Each companion gets a separate case file with separate intake documents
3. **Coordination**: Parent/primary client is expected to facilitate the companion's document signing, provide their information (SSN, medical providers, insurance)
4. **Records Collection**: Each companion's medical providers must be identified and records requested independently — this often falls on the primary client to facilitate
5. **Litigation**: Companions may be joined in one lawsuit (e.g., "Plaintiff A et al. vs. Defendant") or filed separately. Depositions are scheduled sequentially for companions
6. **Settlement**: Each companion's settlement is evaluated independently, but mediation may address all cases simultaneously

## Evidence

- In Case G (MVA), the client's daughter was identified as a companion case during the welcome call. Her Filevine file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. The primary client was asked repeatedly over 6+ months to obtain Children's Hospital records for the companion but failed to do so. Both cases were joined in one lawsuit (Boone 24-CI-00452). During mediation via Zoom, both cases received separate LBF (last best & final) offers. Mediation failed for both.
- In Case I (MVA), a companion witness (Dana Jackson) was deposed alongside the client. Depositions were scheduled sequentially via Zoom — the client was told "we are going to do Dana's deposition first so just chill and I will shoot you a text when we are about done." Coordinating two depositions on the same day required active text communication to manage timing.
- In an MVA case (multi-plaintiff, Floyd County IN), three plaintiffs from the same accident were joined in one lawsuit. The complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action. Deposition scheduling required coordination across all plaintiffs through court reporting services. Mediation was being scheduled for all cases simultaneously. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN), adding jurisdictional complexity for companion case coordination. Two of the settled BI claims were resolved separately per plaintiff, confirming that settlement amounts are independent even in joint litigation.
- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), three additional passengers in the same vehicle were each represented by different law firms (4 total plaintiffs). The at-fault driver's insurance tendered policy limits (25/50) to be divided among all four plaintiffs. The coordination challenge shifted from records collection to inter-counsel distribution: the firm had to email all three other firms to negotiate allocation. This process stalled for months (Jan-Apr 2024+) as counsel for one co-plaintiff was slow to provide medical bills needed for allocation. The delay frustrated the client, who called repeatedly asking for updates and was told "we are still waiting on a reply on the one person we're waiting for medical bills." When distribution could not be agreed upon, the case moved to litigation with all plaintiffs joined in one lawsuit. Depositions for multiple plaintiffs in the joint suit were coordinated through defense counsel.

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake, ask whether other family members were in the vehicle and injured
2. Create separate case files for each companion immediately — do not delay
3. Send intake documents to companions as early as possible
4. Track records collection for each companion independently
5. When the primary client is the gateway to companion information, set explicit deadlines and follow up
6. For litigation, coordinate with the attorney on whether to file jointly or separately
7. Schedule depositions for companions on the same day when possible to minimize coordination burden
8. During mediation, prepare separate settlement evaluations for each companion

## Pitfalls

- Discovering companion cases days/weeks after the primary intake (records requests are delayed)

# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern

> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.

## Pattern

The escalation follows a predictable trajectory:

### Stage 1: Polite Inquiry
- Client texts or calls asking for a general update
- "I would like to know where we are on this case"
- "Just checking in"

### Stage 2: Urgent Demand
- Client's tone shifts to urgency
- "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
- Multiple calls in a single day (3+ calls on same day observed)
- Voicemails become longer and more frustrated

### Stage 3: Threats and Distrust
- Client explicitly threatens to leave
- "I am not settling for what you are"
- "You keep in touch with me or nothing"
- "If you will today, you just please call me back today and just give me something"
- Client may call multiple staff members when the attorney is unavailable

### Stage 4: Repeated Unanswered Attempts
- Client calls are logged as "missed calls" repeatedly over weeks/months
- Voicemail transcriptions become shorter and more resigned
- Client may call different staff members (attorney, paralegal, receptionist)

### Key Triggers
- Long periods of no proactive contact from the firm
- Settlement offer communicated without full explanation

# Unreachable Client Patterns

> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.

## Pattern

### Common Unreachability Signals
1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered

### Multi-Channel Escalation
The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number

### Impact on Case Timeline
- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation

### Client Eventually Resurfaces
Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
- They may provide the requested information belatedly
- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks

# Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) Process

> When the at-fault driver in a Kentucky MVA has no valid insurance coverage (due to coverage denial, policy lapse, or being uninsured), the plaintiff can file a claim through the Kentucky Assigned Claims Plan (KAC). KAC assigns the claim to a carrier (e.g., Nationwide) who acts as the PIP carrier for medical bill payments. The KAC process differs from standard PIP in its documentation requirements, bill submission rules, and the potential for disputes about which bills are covered.

## Pattern

The KAC claim lifecycle:

1. **Identification**: During file setup, when BI coverage is denied or the at-fault driver is uninsured, identify the need for a KAC claim
2. **Filing**: Submit a KAC application with the required documentation (accident report, proof of no coverage, client information)
3. **Assignment**: KAC assigns the claim to a carrier. In observed cases, Nationwide was the assigned carrier.
4. **Adjuster Assignment**: The assigned carrier appoints an adjuster who manages the claim similarly to standard PIP but with additional requirements
5. **Bill Submission**: Medical bills are submitted to the KAC carrier for payment, but the carrier may dispute which bills are covered
6. **Lien Coordination**: KAC payments interact with health insurance liens (e.g., Medicaid/Conduent) — the firm must track what KAC pays versus what the health insurer paid
7. **Coverage Follow-Up**: The KAC carrier will periodically follow up on coverage status — if BI coverage is eventually accepted, the KAC carrier may seek reimbursement

### Key Differences from Standard PIP
- KAC requires the client to have no available PIP coverage of their own OR the at-fault driver to have no coverage
- The assigned carrier performs its own investigation into coverage status
- Bill submission may require proof that the treatment is accident-related (similar to PIP relatedness disputes)
- The attorney may negotiate which bills are submitted to KAC (e.g., agreeing during an EUO that certain bills will not be sent to KAC)
- KAC coverage may have different limits than standard PIP

## Evidence

- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle with coverage denial), a KAC claim was set up through Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company (claim #617417-GM, later #202111021). The at-fault driver's BI carrier (Allstate) denied coverage due to the stolen vehicle claim, triggering the KAC process.
  - The KAC adjuster (Beth Freeman at Nationwide) managed the claim, coordinating with the firm on bill submissions
  - The adjuster called requesting specific documentation: "Requested copy of Conduent Lien, MRs & MBs"
  - The firm "Reviewed & Sorted currently MRs & MB - Replied & provided requested information"
  - During a separate EUO related to the litigation, the attorney agreed that certain bills (from "Starlite Billing") would NOT be submitted to KAC for payment — demonstrating that the KAC process involves negotiation about covered bills
  - The Conduent (Medicaid) lien of $20,669.08 had to be coordinated with KAC payments
  - A "Follow up on coverage" task was set repeatedly (every 1-2 weeks) tracking whether the KAC carrier had confirmed coverage
  - The KAC carrier investigated coverage status in parallel with the BI carrier's coverage investigation

# Medicare Lien Management

> Medicare conditional payment liens represent a federal government interest in recovering payments made for accident-related treatment. Unlike private health insurance subrogation, Medicare liens are governed by the Medicare Secondary Payer (MSP) Act and carry federal legal authority. The presence of a Medicare lien adds complexity to settlement calculations and lien resolution timelines.

## Pattern

### Medicare Lien Lifecycle
1. **Identification**: Medicare is identified as a payer during records/billing review
2. **Conditional Payment Notice**: Medicare/CMS issues a conditional payment letter quantifying amounts paid
3. **Amount Confirmation**: Firm verifies the conditional payment amount against treatment records
4. **Demand Impact**: Medicare lien amount must be factored into demand calculations
5. **Resolution**: After settlement, firm must satisfy Medicare's conditional payment claim before disbursing to client
6. **Potential Negotiation**: Unlike VA liens, Medicare conditional payments may be subject to reduction through the BCRC (Benefits Coordination & Recovery Center) process

### Key Characteristics
- Federal legal authority (MSP Act) — cannot be ignored
- Conditional payment amounts may include treatment unrelated to the accident — requires audit
- CMS/BCRC is the counterparty, not a local office
- Timeline for final demand letter from CMS can extend months post-settlement
- Overlaps with other liens (Medicaid/Wellcare) creating complex multi-lien scenarios

## Evidence

- In Case H (MVA, air evac), a Medicare conditional payment lien was confirmed at $10,157.36 (notice received 5/17/2024). The case also had a Wellcare of Kentucky (Medicaid managed care) lien at $2,315.49 and an unidentified health insurance lien. The multi-lien landscape (Medicare + Medicaid managed care + unknown) created significant complexity for settlement math. The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, meaning Medicare's conditional payment was a fraction of total treatment costs but still substantial enough to impact net recovery.

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake and records review, determine whether the client has Medicare coverage
2. If Medicare paid for any accident-related treatment, initiate a conditional payment query with BCRC early
3. Track the conditional payment amount as a confirmed lien in the case file
4. Audit the conditional payment letter against treatment records — challenge any unrelated charges
5. Factor Medicare lien into demand calculations and settlement evaluation
6. After settlement, submit the final demand to BCRC and negotiate reduction if applicable
7. Do NOT disburse settlement funds until Medicare's final demand is resolved

# Medical Records and Bills Collection

> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.

## Pattern

The records collection workflow follows this sequence:

1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand

## Evidence

- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, 10+ providers), records collection extended over 2+ years. Even during active litigation (July 2025), the firm was still making outbound calls to providers (orthopedics, therapy, PCP) for outstanding records and bills. The medical authorization form was rejected by one provider because it was missing the date of the patient's signature and their DOB — requiring client callback and resubmission, confirming that incomplete authorization forms are a recurring records collection bottleneck. ChartSwap was used as an intermediary for some provider records.
- In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.

# Demand Preparation Workflow

> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.

## Pattern

### Prerequisites for Demand Phase Entry
1. Client confirms treatment is complete (via phone call, documented in log)
2. All known medical providers have been identified
3. Outstanding records/bills requests have been sent
4. Attorney or case manager authorizes the phase change

### Demand Preparation Sequence
1. **Phase Change**: Status changed from "Treatment" to "Demand in Progress" with reason logged
2. **Final Records Requests**: Any outstanding records/bills are requested or re-requested
3. **Records Review**: Medical records are reviewed for:
   - Relatedness to the accident (unrelated treatment must be separated)
   - Completeness (all visits documented)
   - Referrals to other providers discovered in records (new providers must be added)
4. **Lien Identification**: Health insurance liens identified and lien amounts requested (e.g., from Rawlings/WellCare)
5. **Demand Drafting**: Attorney prepares the demand package
6. **Demand Sent**: Package sent to the BI adjuster; follow-up task created

### Records Review Reveals New Providers
A critical pattern: when records from one provider are received and reviewed, they may reference visits to other providers not previously on file. These must be added and their records requested before the demand can proceed.

### Adjuster Proactive Contact During Demand Prep
Insurance adjusters may proactively call to check on claim status during the demand preparation period, especially as the case approaches SOL. This should be documented and the adjuster given a general update without committing to specific timelines.

## Evidence

- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.

# PIP Claims Management

> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.

## Pattern

PIP management follows this lifecycle:

1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid

Key observations from case data:
- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)

## Evidence

- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.
- In Case D, PIP was used to pay a VA lien directly. The firm sent a "direction to pay" the VA lien ($4,437.13) to the PIP carrier along with the VA billing statement. The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs. This demonstrates PIP's utility beyond paying medical providers — it can satisfy government liens.
- In the same case, the PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was "related to a condition she experienced years prior" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness." PIP adjusters perform their own relatedness analysis and will deny bills they consider unrelated to the accident.
- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), two separate PIP claims were set up: one with Farmers Insurance (client's own carrier) and one with American Transit Insurance (NYC cab company involved in a separate incident). The PIP application for Farmers was received and returned in early Dec 2022. The case illustrates managing PIP claims across multiple carriers when there are concurrent accidents, requiring careful tracking of which bills are attributable to which accident for PIP purposes.
- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), PIP was managed through Allstate Insurance, the client's own carrier. The PIP adjuster was contacted separately from the UIM adjuster at the same carrier (different adjuster and claim number). The firm requested the ER bill copy from PIP and saved acknowledgment with the PIP log. This demonstrates the pattern where PIP and UIM are both through the same carrier but managed on separate tracks.
- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), a Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) claim was set up through Nationwide PIP. The KAC process applies when the at-fault driver has no insurance (because coverage was denied due to the stolen vehicle). The PIP adjuster from Nationwide (Beth Freeman) managed the KAC claim, which followed a different process than standard PIP — requiring the firm to submit specific documentation and the adjuster to coordinate with other parties regarding billing disputes. The attorney agreed during an EUO that certain bills would not be submitted to KAC for payment, showing that the KAC process involves negotiation about which bills are covered.", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

   346|### Key New Patterns Identified
   347|- **UIM claims require a separate litigation track**: When BI limits are insufficient, UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer in a separate court, with separate discovery and defense counsel

   350|- **Medicaid liens (Conduent) are actively monitored**: Unlike passive private insurer liens, Conduent calls the firm to check on case status — and Medicaid liens can be the largest single deduction
   351|- **Dental injuries create unique timeline pressure**: Staged implant procedures (18+ months) push cases toward the SOL, often requiring litigation filing before treatment is complete
   352|- **Same carrier for multiple claim types creates confusion**: When PIP, BI, or UIM are at the same carrier, different adjusters handle each track — but the firm must carefully distinguish which claim it's communicating about
   353|- **Pre-settlement funding of $31K+ can exceed net recovery**: In complex cases with large liens and extensive treatment, pre-settlement funding can consume most or all of the client's share

# Case Phase Transitions

> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.

## Pattern

Observed phase transitions across cases:

### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact

### File Setup -> Treatment
- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
- **Timeline**: Days after file setup

### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)

### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation

### Negotiation -> Litigation
- **Trigger**: Inadequate offer, SOL approaching, or adjuster intransigence
- **Prerequisites**: Attorney decision to file, complaint drafted
- **Actions on transition**: File complaint, serve defendants, begin discovery; negotiation may continue in parallel
- **Timeline**: Observed at approximately 1 year post-accident when initial offer was very low

## Evidence

- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.
- In Case D, the case progressed through File Setup (July 2023) -> Treatment (July 2023) -> Negotiation. However, the negotiation phase lasted over 2 years due to VA records delays, multiple adjuster reassignments, and incomplete medical documentation. The attorney eventually ordered "prepare and send the demand" in September 2025 (27 months after the accident) despite incomplete records. This illustrates that VA cases can dramatically extend the typical case timeline, and that at some point the attorney must make a judgment call to proceed with available information rather than waiting for perfection.
- In Case D (auto accident, DOI 2/2023), the case followed the full lifecycle: File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Settlement -> back to Negotiation. The backwards transition from Settlement to Negotiation occurred because the client refused the settlement offer. This shows that phase transitions are not always forward-only.

# Health Insurance Lien Management

> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.

## Pattern

The health insurance lien lifecycle:

1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)

## Evidence

- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA with 20+ providers), the case accumulated 6+ liens including health insurance (Optum at $1,707), provider liens (Cutting Edge Orthopedics at $25,763.75), and a hospital funding lien (Plano Surgical Hospital Funding LLC at $2,553.70). Multiple "Unknown" lien holders were also identified, illustrating that lien tracking becomes exponentially harder with more providers. Provider liens (as distinct from health insurance liens) may come from providers who treated on a lien basis.
- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid both asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm used ZipLiens to track the health insurance lien but initially sent the request to the wrong entity. Delta Dental of Kentucky also asserted a lien (amount unconfirmed). This demonstrates that dental injury cases can involve dental insurance liens in addition to health insurance liens — a distinct lien type not seen in standard MVA cases.
- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), Humana asserted a health insurance subrogation lien of $11,809. The case also involved pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners), illustrating how the combination of health insurance liens plus funding can dramatically reduce client net recovery.
- In Case H (auto accident, Medicaid), a Conduent lien of $20,669.08 was identified. Conduent is the third-party administrator for Kentucky Medicaid subrogation. The firm sent a first lien letter to Conduent at intake and later provided the Conduent lien information to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. Conduent called the firm to follow up on the case status, demonstrating that Medicaid subrogation administrators actively monitor cases.
- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), multiple health insurance liens were identified early: Anthem BCBS, a managed care provider, and a pain management provider lien. The lien request was faxed to the health insurer on the day of file setup. Additionally, multiple "Unknown" lien holders appeared in the lien tracker, demonstrating that in cases with 6+ medical providers, identifying all subrogation interests takes extended effort. The case's policy limits (25/50 split among 4 plaintiffs) made lien resolution particularly critical since the per-plaintiff recovery was limited.
- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), a health insurance lien was identified from Humana at $5,887.97 (amount confirmed). Three additional "Unknown" lien holders were also listed. The Humana lien was identified early in the case via the case summary. The confirmed lien amount combined with the estate nature of the case (deceased client) means lien resolution will impact the surviving spouse's net recovery. This case further confirms the pattern that health insurance liens must be identified at intake and tracked throughout the case — even in litigation that spans 5+ years.

## Agent Guidance

1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially

## Pitfalls

- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed

# Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management

> When the at-fault driver's BI policy limits are insufficient to cover the plaintiff's damages, the plaintiff may pursue an Underinsured Motorist (UIM) claim against their own auto insurance carrier. UIM claims run as a parallel track to the BI claim and require distinct procedural steps including consent-to-settle notifications, separate complaint filing, and independent adjuster management — often with the same carrier that provides the client's PIP coverage.

## Pattern

UIM claim management follows a parallel-track structure alongside the BI claim:

### Setup
1. **Identify UIM Coverage**: During file setup, check whether the client's own auto policy includes UIM coverage. If the at-fault driver's BI limits are low relative to the client's injuries, UIM becomes relevant.
2. **Separate Claim Setup**: The UIM claim requires its own claim number and adjuster, even when the UIM carrier is the same company providing PIP.
3. **Consent to Settle**: Before accepting the BI settlement, the UIM carrier must be notified and given an opportunity to consent (or subrogate). This is a critical procedural requirement.

### Parallel Management
- The UIM adjuster is separate from the PIP adjuster at the same carrier
- The UIM claim number may be the same as or different from the PIP claim number
- The UIM complaint is filed in the appropriate court (may be different jurisdiction than BI)
- UIM litigation follows standard discovery and deposition procedures
- The UIM carrier files an answer to the complaint and may retain defense counsel

### Litigation
- UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer — an adversarial posture against the client's own carrier
- A courtesy copy of the complaint should be sent to the UIM adjuster
- Discovery includes the plaintiff's first set of interrogatories/production requests to the UIM carrier
- UIM litigation can proceed independently from BI settlement

## Evidence

- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), the client's extensive injuries (including $45,830+ hospital bills, physical therapy, chiropractic care, and surgery) exceeded the at-fault driver's BI policy limits (State Farm, claim #1754K417G). The client had UIM coverage through Allstate Insurance (claim #723447272). The UIM claim required separate management:
  - PIP adjuster (Margaret Sharp) handled PIP claim through Allstate
  - UIM adjuster (Nick Burchette) handled the UIM claim separately
  - A UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580, "McCay v. Allstate Property and Casualty Insurance")
  - Allstate filed an answer to the complaint
  - A consent-to-settle form was obtained from Allstate before the BI claim could proceed
  - Discovery commenced with plaintiff's first set of discovery to Allstate
  - The case involved $319.82 in court filing fees, plus expenses for EvenUP ($500) and Digital Owl ($495) for case evaluation
  - The client also had a Humana health insurance lien ($11,809) and pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners)
  - Defense counsel (Jennifer Ann Peterson) was retained by Allstate
  - A separate "waiving subrogation" document was filed

## Agent Guidance

1. At intake, identify the client's own auto insurance UIM coverage limits

# Treatment Gaps Affect Settlement Value

> This connection documents how [[treatment-gap-defense]] interacts with [[client-expectations-management]] and [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] to create cases where the client receives minimal net recovery despite having a valid claim.

## Connection

Two concepts converge to create low-value outcomes:

1. **Treatment Gap Defense** ([[treatment-gap-defense]]): The adjuster uses the gap in treatment to justify a lower offer. The client may have stopped treatment due to practical reasons (incarceration, work/parenting constraints) rather than lack of injury.

2. **Client Declines Treatment** ([[client-declines-recommended-treatment]]): When a doctor recommends follow-up care (chiropractic, PT) but the client does not follow through, it creates the very treatment gap the adjuster will exploit.

The interaction creates a vicious cycle: the client's circumstances prevent treatment, the lack of treatment lowers the case value, and the lower case value means less recovery for a client who may need it most.

## Evidence

- In Case A, the client had only one treatment provider and a significant gap. The adjuster cited the gap to justify the offer. After subtracting pre-settlement funding ($6,537) and reduced medical bills ($5,397), the client would net less than $3K from an $18K offer.
- In Case B, the client's doctor recommended chiropractic care, but the client could not coordinate treatment around work and parenting. The spouse acknowledged this was "an area of risk."

## Insight

Early intervention on treatment gaps could significantly improve case value. When a client indicates they may stop treatment, the firm should:
1. Document the practical barriers to treatment
2. Counsel the client on the impact of stopping treatment
3. Help find practical solutions (providers with flexible hours, telehealth options)
4. If treatment truly cannot continue, document the reasons thoroughly for the demand narrative

## Agent Guidance

1. Monitor treatment continuity — flag any gap exceeding 14 days
2. When a client indicates they want to stop treatment, escalate to the attorney
3. In the demand letter, proactively explain any treatment gap with supporting documentation
4. Track the treatment gap + financial obligations equation to predict net client recovery early

## Related

- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — the adjuster tactic this connection feeds
- [[client-expectations-management]] — clients need to understand the impact
- [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] — funding compounds the low-value problem
